discovery_name: GLP-1 Weight Loss Drugs (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro)

tracker_summary.invention: GLP-1 receptor agonist drugs for type 2 diabetes and obesity
tracker_summary.inventor: Chain began with Joel Habener (GLP-1 discovery), John Eng (exendin-4 isolation), and was commercialized through Amylin Pharmaceuticals and later Novo Nordisk/Eli Lilly
tracker_summary.date: Scientific chain: 1967–2024; First FDA approval (exenatide): 2005; Semaglutide for obesity: 2021
tracker_summary.current_use: Treatment of type 2 diabetes and obesity; active investigation for Alzheimer's disease, addiction, NASH, and cardiovascular disease
tracker_summary.initial_funding_source: U.S. Veterans Administration (VA) and National Institutes of Health (NIH)
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Basic research into venom peptides as probes for mammalian physiology; diabetes research at VA hospitals

the_x: NIH-funded basic research into Gila monster lizard venom peptides, conducted at a VA hospital with catalog-ordered reptiles
the_y: A $100+ billion global market for GLP-1 obesity drugs, with emerging applications in Alzheimer's disease, addiction, and metabolic disorders
summary: In the 1980s, a VA endocrinologist named John Eng, working in a small laboratory at the Bronx Veterans Affairs Medical Center, ordered Gila monster venom from a biological supply catalog on a hunch that reptile venoms might contain peptides that mimic or illuminate human gut hormones. After years of painstaking isolation work, Eng identified a peptide he called exendin-4, which turned out to be a near-perfect mimic of the human hormone GLP-1 — but dramatically more stable. When Eng tried to patent the discovery through the VA, the institution declined. Eng patented it himself, presented a poster at an ADA conference, and a small San Diego biotech saw it. Thirty years later, 9% of the U.S. population is projected to be on GLP-1 drugs by the end of the decade.

chain.who: Rosalyn Yalow, Joel Habener, Graeme Bell
chain.where: Bronx VA; Massachusetts General Hospital; University of Chicago
chain.what_they_were_doing: Yalow's VA radioimmunoassay enabled gut hormone detection; Habener cloned proglucagon, discovering GLP-1.
chain.the_jump: GLP-1 emerged not from diabetes research but from gene sequencing done with no therapeutic target in mind.

chain.who: Jens Juul Holst; Svetlana Mojsov / Joel Habener
chain.where: University of Copenhagen; Massachusetts General Hospital
chain.what_they_were_doing: GLP-1 stimulates insulin only when blood sugar is elevated — theoretically impossible to cause dangerous hypoglycemia.
chain.the_jump: This glucose-dependent safety profile reframed GLP-1 from biological curiosity to therapeutic target.
chain.funding: Danish Medical Research Council; NIH; Novo Nordisk

chain.what_they_were_doing: Ordered Gila monster venom from a catalog; hypothesized an animal eating few times yearly evolved extreme metabolic peptides.
chain.the_jump: Eng isolated exendin-4 — 53% homology to GLP-1, resistant to degradation, far longer half-life than any mammalian equivalent.

chain.what_they_were_doing: VA declined to patent exendin-4; Eng filed the patent out of pocket and presented findings as a conference poster.
chain.the_jump: Had Eng not personally filed, exendin-4 enters the public domain with no commercialization pathway. The VA's refusal accidentally preserved the innovation.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Young saw Eng's poster, recognized exendin-4's stability, licensed the patent to begin development as exenatide.
chain.the_jump: A single conference poster was the entire commercialization mechanism — no formal tech transfer, no institutional deal.

chain.where: San Diego; Indianapolis; Silver Spring MD
chain.what_they_were_doing: Amylin nearly went bankrupt before Eli Lilly partnership rescued trials; FDA approved exenatide (Byetta) April 2005.
chain.the_jump: Weight loss appeared as an unexpected secondary finding in trial data — the 'side effect' that would become a hundred-billion-dollar indication.

chain.who: Lotte Bjerre Knudsen, Novo Nordisk; STEP trial investigators
chain.what_they_were_doing: Fatty acid modification enabled once-weekly dosing; STEP 1 showed 14.9% body weight reduction — unprecedented without surgery.
chain.the_jump: STEP trials reframed obesity as a biological disease with a pharmaceutical solution, triggering a global paradigm shift.

chain.who: NIH National Institute on Aging; Novo Nordisk
chain.what_they_were_doing: GLP-1 receptors found widely in the brain; users showed lower Alzheimer's rates; Phase 3 trials launched.
chain.the_jump: NIH-funded venom peptide biology now funds brain disease trials — the same programs that mapped peptide receptors in the 1970s underpin the Alzheimer's hypothesis.
